questions.questionText: A test with high sensitivity is best used for:
questions.optionB: Screening (ruling OUT disease — SnNOut: a Sensitive test, when Negative, rules Out the disease)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Sensitive test + negative result = disease ruled out (SnNOut). Why correct: High sensitivity → few false negatives → negative result reliably excludes disease → ideal for SCREENING. High specificity → few false positives → positive result reliably confirms disease (SpPIn) → ideal for CONFIRMATION. Why A wrong: Confirming diagnosis needs high SPECIFICITY (SpPIn). Why C wrong: Prognosis depends on staging/other factors. Why D wrong: Treatment response = clinical endpoints. Clinical relevance: Screening tests: D-dimer for PE (high sensitivity, low specificity) — if negative, PE excluded. Confirmatory: Western blot for HIV (high specificity). Exam trap: Increasing sensitivity usually decreases specificity (trade-off — moving cutoff point). ROC curve plots this trade-off. Memory aid: 'SnNOut: Sensitive Negative rules Out. SpPIn: Specific Positive rules In.' Reference: Park – Biostatistics.

questions.questionText: The p-value in a hypothesis test represents:
questions.optionA: The probability that the alternative hypothesis is true
questions.optionB: The probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true (p < 0.05 → reject null → statistically significant)
questions.optionC: The probability that the null hypothesis is true
questions.explanation: Core concept: p-value = probability of observing the data (or more extreme) IF the null is true. Why correct: If p < 0.05 (conventional threshold): results unlikely under null → reject null → 'statistically significant.' p-value is NOT the probability that null is true or that results are due to chance. Why A wrong: p-value doesn't directly give probability of alternative hypothesis being true (that requires Bayesian analysis). Why C wrong: Common misconception. p-value ≠ P(H0 true). Why D wrong: Power = 1 - β (probability of detecting true effect, related to sample size). Clinical relevance: Type I error (α): rejecting true null (false positive). Type II error (β): failing to reject false null (false negative). Power = 1-β. Exam trap: p < 0.05 = statistically significant but NOT necessarily CLINICALLY significant — effect size matters. Memory aid: 'p < 0.05 = Probably not by chance (reject null).' Reference: Park – Biostatistics.

questions.questionText: Which measure of central tendency is least affected by outliers/extreme values?
questions.optionB: Median (middle value when data arranged in order — robust to outliers and used for skewed distributions)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Median is the most robust measure of central tendency against outliers. Why correct: Median = middle value (50th percentile). Not influenced by extreme values (outliers). Used for skewed distributions (income, length of stay). Why A wrong: Mean is MOST affected by outliers (pulled toward extreme values). Used for normally distributed data. Why C wrong: Mode (most frequent value) — not affected by outliers but less informative for continuous data. Why D wrong: Range (max-min) is a measure of DISPERSION, not central tendency, and is very sensitive to outliers. Clinical relevance: Normal distribution: mean = median = mode. Right-skewed (positive): mean > median > mode. Left-skewed (negative): mean < median < mode. Exam trap: For normally distributed data, mean is preferred. For skewed data, use median. Know which to use when. Memory aid: 'Median = Middle = not Moved by extremes.' Reference: Park – Biostatistics.
questions.conceptTag: Median robust outliers central tendency skewed
